coredata.dc:description: Diabetes is still one of the leading causes of death worldwide. According to the World Health Organization, around 1.5 million people worldwide died due to diabetes in 2019. It is estimated that 463 million people are living with diabetes all over the world. Many successful studies reported that splendid antihyperglycemic drugs can be developed with the help of new technologies and they can effectively deliver the active molecules by nanocarriers. In particular, recent studies show that some nanostructures namely quantum dots can be taken orally and their blood glucose-reducing effects are noteworthy. In our previous studies insulin was found to lower blood glucose levels when administered orally in liposome formulations. This study, it was aimed to examine whether carbon quantum dots can be used to transport insulin through the oral route and whether can they protect insulin from gastrointestinal enzymes as they can easily pass through both cell membranes and biological walls. It is also aimed to reveal the mechanism by which this effect occurs. First off, fluorescent composite carbon quantum dots (N-doped) smaller than 10 nm were prepared with l-cysteine, treated with insulin, and decorated with poly ethylene glycol 3350 and methylcellulose for oral administration. As a result, these developed carbon quantum dots were administered orally to diabetic animals and their blood sugars were reduced by about 60% within 5 h. It was then found that their blood levels were similarly reduced when prepared composite carbon quantum dots were orally administered alone to rats. It is found that although they do not contain any insulin, they increase the cellular response and metabolism in the mitochondria, causing glycolysis and thus having an antihyperglycemic effect when they are given orally. This is the first study in the literature that evaluated that the fine structure of the carbon quantum dot is important for having an antihyperglycemic effect and the l-quantum dot structure will be more effective.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Diabetes is still one of the leading causes of death worldwide. According to the World Health Organization, around 1.5 million people worldwide died due to diabetes in 2019. It is estimated that 463 million people are living with diabetes all over the world. Many successful studies reported that splendid antihyperglycemic drugs can be developed with the help of new technologies and they can effectively deliver the active molecules by nanocarriers. In particular, recent studies show that some nanostructures namely quantum dots can be taken orally and their blood glucose-reducing effects are noteworthy. In our previous studies insulin was found to lower blood glucose levels when administered orally in liposome formulations. This study, it was aimed to examine whether carbon quantum dots can be used to transport insulin through the oral route and whether can they protect insulin from gastrointestinal enzymes as they can easily pass through both cell membranes and biological walls. It is also aimed to reveal the mechanism by which this effect occurs.

First off, fluorescent composite carbon quantum dots (N-doped) smaller than 10 nm were prepared with l-cysteine, treated with insulin, and decorated with poly ethylene glycol 3350 and methylcellulose for oral administration. As a result, these developed carbon quantum dots were administered orally to diabetic animals and their blood sugars were reduced by about 60% within 5 h.

It was then found that their blood levels were similarly reduced when prepared composite carbon quantum dots were orally administered alone to rats. It is found that although they do not contain any insulin, they increase the cellular response and metabolism in the mitochondria, causing glycolysis and thus having an antihyperglycemic effect when they are given orally. This is the first study in the literature that evaluated that the fine structure of the carbon quantum dot is important for having an antihyperglycemic effect and the l-quantum dot structure will be more effective.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Diabetes Mellitus (DM), which is among the top five diseases that cause death in the world, is a disease that directly affects human health, the workforce, and the success of society. The incidence of diseases also increases with the increase in the average age of society. The number of people with diabetes is increasing day by day due to the increase in the world population, aging, urbanization, obesity prevalence, and physical inactivity. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), the prevalence of diabetes, which affects approximately 422 million people worldwide, is predicted to double in the next 20 years [1]. DM is a metabolic disease that occurs as a result of decreased secretion or activity of insulin (INS) released from pancreatic beta cells of the Langerhans islet. At the same time, this disease, which requires constant care, is characterized by the body's inability to make use of carbohydrates, fats, and proteins sufficiently and glucose excretion from the urine [2,3]. Glucose is the most important monosaccharide in human blood and provides energy for tissues. In diabetes, regular glycemic control against increased glucose is essential. INS replacement therapy is the most preferred treatment for diabetes and provides good glycemic control. Although it is possible to classify diabetes as “INS-dependent diabetes” and “non–INS–dependent diabetes”, it is not used much because it reflects a treatment-based classification and causes confusion. Type I and Type II diabetes definitions are used instead. Apart from these, there are other types of diabetes that are observed for a wide variety of reasons [4].

Type I diabetes, in other words, INS-dependent diabetes, occurs as a result of the destruction of beta cells that secrete INS from the pancreatic islet. As a result of the inability to produce INS after the destruction of beta cells, glucose cannot enter the cells and its accumulation in the blood is observed. In Type II diabetes, another type of diabetes, INS resistance is observed in muscle, fat, and liver, and its incidence is ten times higher than in Type I. Type II diabetes is observed when INS in the body cannot be used effectively. In both types, there is INS deficiency or absence as the beta cells of the islet of the Langerhans cells are damaged [5–7]. In addition, people with a predisposition may also have gestational diabetes during pregnancy due to some hormones produced during pregnancy and the increase in metabolic load. In this type of diabetes, blood sugar usually returns to normal after pregnancy. However, approximately 40% of these people may develop Type II diabetes within the next 15 [4,7]. Disturbances in energy metabolism are observed in both types. All of these changes can cause secondary diseases such as cardiovascular disorders, diabetic retinopathy, nephropathy, and neuropathy [8].

INS is a major, polypeptide anabolic hormone that is released from the beta cells of the pancreas and plays a role with glucagon in the regulation of carbohydrate and fat metabolism in the body, used in the treatment of Type 1 and Type 2 diabetes [7]. INS molecule is formed as a result of connecting A and B polypeptide chains containing 21 and 30 amino acids to each other by disulfide bonds [9].

This structure, which can be found as a monomer, dimer, or hexamer, can turn into a dimer or hexamer by combining with ionic interactions according to ambient conditions [10]. INS, which has a molecular weight of approximately 6 kDa, is a protein consisting of 51 amino acids and is used for parenteral administration in the treatment of diabetes [11].

INS ensures that the free glucose in the blood enters the cell, keeping the blood sugar level at a normal level and providing energy for life. However, in diabetic patients, the pancreas's inability to produce enough INS or the inability to use the produced INS effectively in the body causes blood sugar to rise. In this case, INS, which is of vital importance for the body, must be supplied externally [12].

Diabetes patients constantly need INS injections, but this is both painful and causes various damage at the injection site. The INS molecule, which has a peptide structure, is rapidly degraded when given orally. Therefore, the ailment is effective and reliable; it is necessary to provide treatment with a dosage form that is easily acceptable to patients. Based on this, oral administration of INS is thought to be a solution that can dramatically improve the quality of life of these patients.

Like diabetes mellitus, patients having gestational diabetes are also subjected to many drugs development studies to improve their quality of life. It is reported in the literature that treatments are not suitable for the prevention and management of patients with gestational diabetes mellitus. As an alternative therapeutic opportunity and a leading scientific technique, nanotechnology namely theragnostic nanomaterials with unique properties and small sizes have been recently engineered for clinics and pharmaceutics. Reducing materials to the nanoscale has successfully changed their properties and enabled them to uniquely interact with cell biomolecules. Several biosensing methods have been developed to monitor glucose levels in gestational diabetes mellitus patients. Many nanoparticles like cerium oxide, selenium, polymeric nanoparticles, and drug-loaded nanoparticles loaded with therapeutic agents have been used for gestational diabetes mellitus treatment. Still, there are some challenges associated with the detection limits and toxicity of such nanomaterials. This review covers the aspects of a fast-developing field of generating nanomaterials and their applications in gestational diabetes mellitus diagnosis and treatment. This review highlights the importance of the size of the delivery system [13].

Extracellular vesicles are heterogeneous membranous vesicles secreted by living cells that are involved in many physiological and pathological processes as intermediaries for intercellular communication and molecular transfer. Recent studies have shown that these vesicles can regulate the occurrence and development of tumors by transferring proteins, lipids, and nucleic acids to immune cells as signaling molecules. As a new diagnostic biomarker and drug delivery system, extracellular vesicles have broad application prospects in immunotherapy. The breakthrough of nanotechnology or designing and physical characteristics of the delivery system has been highlighted to be important and it has promoted the development and exploration of engineered extracellular vesicles for immune-targeted therapy [14]. In this review, the uniqueness of extracellular vesicles in immune regulation and the engineering strategies used for immunotherapy highlight the logic of their design through typical examples. The authors discussed the present situation and challenges of clinical transformation and the development prospects of nanovesicles in immunotherapy. The goal of this review is to explain some new insights into the design of the immune-regulatory of these extracellular vesicles and the possibilities to expand their application in cancer immunotherapy. The structure of the delivery system clearly appears to be an important parameter for the design.

The importance of the molecular structure, even the chirality found to be important and the design may not be perfectly done because of a lack of deep knowledge to understand or predict specific biological activities of peptide or peptide-like molecules when a nano delivery system is used. It is reported in the literature that a “mirror-image peptide grafting” method to graft the epitopes of bioactive d-peptide onto the mini protein template to construct a self-assembled supra-particle was achieved [15]. By means of a grafting, an enantiomeric peptide functioned as an agonist, onto Apamin, and successfully constructed a self-assembled d-enantiomeric mini protein supermolecule nanoparticle was prepared. This chiral supraparticle possesses a favorable pharmaceutical profile including passive tumor targeting, cell membrane penetration, intracellular reductive responsiveness, and endosome escaping. These particles showed in vitro and in vivo antiproliferative activity and augmented antitumor immunity. This enabling strategy is reported to allow the fabrication of a class of peptide/protein-derived supramolecular chirality with predictable biological activities and will likely have a broad impact on the chiral nanotechnology at the service of prevention and treatment of human diseases.

The structure, the design and the physical properties are important parameters for the effect. These parameters affect the mechanism of effect. In our previous studies, regarding the oral use of INS in liposomes were conducted and some positive results were obtained [16]. In these trials, it was determined that the INS molecule was able to go to the lymph channel through “Peyer's Plaques” or that INS changed its three-dimensional structure by restructuring the liposome formulation. Thus, the INS molecule was able to penetrate more rapidly and the blood glucose level could be lowered, and this was interpreted for the first time in the literature by relating it to liposome formulations [16]. During these studies, it was observed that the preparation conditions greatly affected the structure of INS and the effect was greater as the liposomes were reduced. It was noted that a higher effect was observed with the use of carboxy methyl cellulose, which increased viscosity and made the system bio-adhesive. It was aimed to transport INS by binding to a hydrophobic, small and surface-modifiable carrier, and for this purpose, Fullerene was chosen as a carbon structure in the form of a ball of nanometer size. In this study, with different model substances, the passage of various drug molecules given with fullerene through the model membranes was investigated. It has been determined that fullerene can adsorption and desorption according to the physicochemical state of the active substance, and its surface is suitable for various modifications and functions [17]. However, since fullerene was obtained ready-made, fullerene could be obtained in a combined state, and the inability to open this union under laboratory conditions caused aggregation problems and as a result, it was not possible to obtain small nanostructures. For this reason, it was thought to work with carbon nanotubes and many studies were conducted on how to use carbon nanotubes as carriers with different active substances [18–23]. In these studies, it has been demonstrated for the first time in the literature that when carbon nanotubes are used, molecules pass through biological membranes at a higher rate and these effects are seen due to the change in the thermodynamic properties of molecules [20]. Detailed research on how carbon surfaces can be modified and how carbon surface morphology changes when modified with polyethylene glycol (PEG), and how, the reactions caused by carbon surfaces in the body can be reduced by our group [21]. In later studies, it was investigated whether the events on the surface and the properties of drug molecule retention come from carbon or surface effect because they are very small particles [24–26]. In another study conducted for this purpose, it has been demonstrated that when boron nitride nanotubes or quantum dots are used, similar surface effects and storage effects can be obtained, and even better biological compatibility and higher penetration effect of quantum dots are observed [27]. Zinc oxide (ZnO) was used as the quantum dot, but the adsorbing properties of the drug molecule were found to be somewhat weak [25]. In addition, it has been very difficult to load active substances into ZnO and it has a toxic effect above a certain dose. For this reason, very small-sized carrier systems with adsorption properties such as carbon have been investigated. Meanwhile, new information about the production of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) has been found in the literature and studies have been started on the production of CQDs [28–31]. Because it has been understood that a carrier system to be prepared with composite carbon quantum dots (CCQDs) will be very useful for oral delivery of INS.

Quantum dots (QDs) are semiconductor nanocrystals with a few atomic sizes obtained from compounds between II-VI or III-V in the periodic table, which are very advantageous in terms of optical and chemical properties. Although their size depends on the materials used in their preparation, they usually vary between 2 and 10 nm [32,33]. These structures, which can be metallic or semiconductor, show quantum effects due to their very small dimensions, give a narrow band emission in the wide ultraviolet (UV) absorption spectrum, and show strong fluorescence and high photostability [34,35]. These features allow QDs to monitor the cell in vivo with imaging methods without the need to kill the animal during biomedical applications such as bioimaging, sensors and diagnostics, and in vivo animal experiments. At the same time, QDs become potential because they can be detected with high sensitivity in ex-vivo imaging techniques [34,36,37].

Studies have shown that metallic quantum dots are problematic for use in humans due to their toxicity [28]. The result of carbon quantum dots (CCQDs), on the other hand, have excellent optical properties of conventional semiconductor quantum dots, as well as compensate for the shortcomings of conventional materials in terms of cytotoxicity, environmental and biological hazards In addition, CCQDs have properties such as good solubility in water, chemical stability, and easy surface functionalization [38]. Several recent papers show that carbon quantum dots can enhance INS stabilization, inhibiting certain inactivation processes such as fibrillation or misfolding [39].

Our previous studies with nanostructures and liposomes showed that when INS is administered through the oral route their structures and possible entering site in the body play an important role [16]. We found that when INS molecule present in the nanostructure they can change their 3Dimensional structure and maybe become smaller in the size and if they are in an appropriate size and hydrophilicity they can prefer to go into the lymphatic system where enzymatic degradation may not be very fast and high [16]. Moreover, recent research results indicated that chiral and N-doped carbon quantum dots prepared with cysteine can affect energy metabolism by targeting mitochondria in the cells [40]. Therefore, it was aimed to prepare composite carbon quantum dots with INS to investigate the possible mechanism and to understand effective parameters and necessary structure for optimizing the antihyperlipidemic effect.

In this study, citric acid monohydrate and l-cysteine were chosen for carbon source and surface passivation. CCQDs produced using a microwave synthesis device were treated with monomeric INS to ensure that the INS molecule adhered to the surface. The surface modification performed here allows INS to become more effective and to act by passing through biological membranes without deterioration when given orally. With the surface modifications made with PEG, the most durable INS CCQDs formulation was determined and the particle size was measured as 2–10 nm. When the prepared formulations were examined under 365 nm UV light, it was shown that it showed intense and bright blue fluorescence, slowed the release of INS from the formulation, and was effective in lowering blood glucose levels in animals with oral administration. Thus, it can be a source for studies on the transport and targeting of CCQDs and drug molecules.

It is aimed to understand the physical appearances, particle size, zeta potential, fluorescence properties, and the quantum yield of the CCQDs-INS in this study. It is also examined whether these CCQDs can be used in INS transport and protected from gastrointestinal enzymes since they can easily pass through both cell membranes and biological walls. The INS loading capacity to CCQDs, INS release, and the release kinetic from CCQDs were studied and determined. The possible pathways or mechanisms were also discussed. The stability was also tested to find out the stability by measuring the drug level and to guarantee that enough INS was present in the formulation.

Citric acid monohydrate, polyethylene glycol 3350, and methylcellulose (4000 cp) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (Sigma Aldrich Chemie GmbH, Eschenstr.5 82024 Taufkirchen Germany). l-cysteine was from Biobasic (20 Konrad Crescent, Markham ON, L3R 8T4, Canada). Streptozocin was obtained from Merck (126 East Lincoln Avenue, P.O. Box 2000, Rahway, NJ 07065 USA). Novarapid-Flexpen was used as an INS source. A microwave reactor (Microwave 300, Anton Paar, St. Albans Hertfordshire, AL4 0LA, UK) was used for the synthesis of CCQDs. Anton Paar LiteSizer500 device was used for characterization (particle size and distribution, zeta potential measurements). Electron microscope (High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HR-TEM) Carl Zeis EM 900, Carl-Zeiss-Straße 22, Oberkochen, 73447, Germany) was used to determine the physical appearance of CCQDs. Franz type of diffusion cells and Enzyme – Linked Immunosorbent Assay (Elisa) Insulin kit (Mouse-rat Insulin kit, CAYMAN Chemical, 1180 East Ellsworth Road, Ann Harbor, Michigan 48108, USA) were used for the analysis.

Spectrofluorometer (Model 229129, Agilent Technologies, 5301 Stevens Creek Blvd., Santa Clara, CA 95051, USA) was used to determine the optical properties of CCQDs. Wistar rats were used for in vivo experiments. Experiments were performed in accordance with the permission obtained from the ethics committee of Ankara University Faculty of Pharmacy, Department of Pharmacology (Ankara University, Local Ethics Committee for Animal Experiments, decision no: 2017-10-90). This study also followed Animal Research: Reporting In Vivo Experiments (ARRIVE) guidelines. ANOVA test was used for comparisons and statistical evaluations.

The number of rats included in the experimental procedure is 36 rats. The inclusion criteria are white male Wistar rats, the age is 3–6 months, and the rats should be healthy and show active movements. The exclusion criteria are rats that show aggressiveness, attacked other rats, and sick during adaptation. The drop-out criteria are rats that died after induction of Streptozocin and died during the experimental research and monitoring. We do the randomization using computer randomization to avoid any subjectivity. The researchers that gave the drugs to the rats were already trained and had the capabilities to do the experimental procedures. All drugs that were given were already measured and made into the same amount, 1 ml, so the researchers didn't know which drugs or group they gave.

The conditions and used parameters were found to be effective on the dimensions and surface properties of CCQDs. Therefore the production method was optimized [41]. To obtain N-doped composite CCQDs, 0.4 g of citric acid monohydrate was used as a carbon source and 0.005 g of l-cysteine was used for the amine addition to the structure and the surface passivation. All chemicals were dissolved in 1 ml of distilled water and then transferred to the microwave reactor. The heating program was set to the following parameters:
-
Heating up to 140 °C: 5 min.
-
Maintaining temperature at 140 °C: 20 min.
-
Cooling to room temperature.

At the end of the reaction procedure, CCQDs were obtained. The production of CCQDs was confirmed by UV light (365 nm). A bright blue emission was observed and then an ml of INS solution (100 IU/ml). 0.02 g/ml methyl cellulose (4000 cp) and PEG 3350 were added to the CCQDs solution at room temperature. The mixture was gently stirred for an overnight period at room temperature. The final CCQDs were checked again under UV light (365 nm) for the last quick confirmation.

It was noticed that when Na-Citrate was used instead of citric acid the green fluorescence was observed under UV light (365 nm) but blue light emitting CCQDs were used for further in-vitro and in-vivo experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical appearances, particle size, and zeta potential
ce:section.ce:para: Structure and morphology analysis have been performed on CCQDs. The particle size and distribution were determined using Anton Paar Lite Sizer 500. The test was repeated before and after INS addition. All measurements were repeated (n = 6 and n = 12 for particle size and zeta potential measurements respectively).
Spectro fluorimetry was used to determine the optical properties of CCQDs. CCQDs were excited and their emissions were recorded. Their appearances under UV light were also controlled and framed.

The method was adapted from the literature [42]. Briefly, the absorbance of quinine sulfate in sulfuric acid solution was determined at 350 nm. The full fluorescence spectrum (fluorescent intensity versus wavelength) of the solution was obtained. The solution was diluted (as 0%, 20%, 40%, 60%, 80%, and 100% of original solution) and their fluorescence spectrums were plotted. The areas under these curves (AUC) were calculated using the trapezoidal method. The AUC values were plotted against intensities. The slope value was determined. The same procedure was used for the CCQDs solution and a similar fluorescence spectrum was also obtained. The quantum yield was calculated as follows:
Quantum yield = {m(test)/m(quinine sulfate)}*A
A= (refractive index-water/refractive index-0.1 M sulfuric acid) = 0.99939985.
[Refractive index-water = 1.3325; refractive index-0.1 M sulfuric acid = 1.3333].
m: slope of area under curve (AUC) - intensity graphic.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of insulin loading capacity to CCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The test was performed using an Elisa rat insulin kit. Briefly, after the loading of INS to the CCQDs, the solution was subjected to the test. Solutions were centrifuged for an hour and aliquots were removed, remaining CCQDs were analyzed and the loading capacity of prepared CCQDs was determined.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of drug release and release kinetics of insulin from CCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Cellophane membrane and Franz diffusion cells were used. The donor part was filled with an ml of CQDs and 7.25 mL of phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.4) was placed in the receptor compartment. The experiment was conducted at 37 °C. 100 μL samples were taken at certain times and immediately replenished with fresh solution. The samples were then analyzed with the Elisa kit. Finally, the INS release and release kinetics were determined. All experiments were replicated six times and results were given as mean ± SD. The kinetic of the release was then determined considering r2 values.

The number of rats included in the experimental procedure is 36 rats. The inclusion criteria are white male Rattus novergicus strain Wistar, the age is 3–6 months, and the rats should be healthy and show active movements. The exclusion criteria are rats that show aggressiveness, attacked other rats, and sick during adaptation. The drop-out criteria are rats that died after induction of streptozocin and died during the experimental research and monitoring. All rats are from Ankara University, Faculty of Pharmacy, Department of Pharmacology. Before we do the induction and made the rats diabetic, we do the adaptation for 7 days, we gave foods and drinks based on the standard of Association of Analytical Communities (AOAC) 2005, which contains carbohydrates, protein 10%, fat 3%, minerals, vitamins dan water 12%. We do the randomization using computer randomization to avoid any subjectivities. We put the rats in the 50 × 50 × 20 cm3 based on randomization. We kept the rats in the Laboratory of Pharmacology at Ankara University. Rats were made diabetic with streptozocin (STZ, 40 mg/kg dose was given intravenously). The researchers that gave the drugs to the rats were already trained and had the capabilities to do the experimental procedures. A week after the STZ injection, the plasma glucose levels were measured. Diabetic animals were divided into 6 groups (control, positive control, oral CCQDs-INS 100 IU, oral CCQDs-INS 50 IU, oral bare CCQDs applied group, and formulation components). All drugs that were given were already measured and made into the same amount, 1 ml, so the researchers didn't know which drugs or group they gave. Their blood glucose level was monitored and plotted after oral administration of formulations by gavage. Their blood samples of around 100 μl were collected from their tails by a small cut at 0, 15, 30, 60, 90, 120, and 180 min. Then the blood was centrifugated and the plasma glucose levels were measured.
Stability tests were performed to determine the drug level and to guarantee that enough INS was present in the formulation. The chemical, physical stability, and shelf life were determined.

The average values based on the experiment for evaluating the results are given by specifying the mean ± standard deviation, also the number of iterations. A two-way ANOVA test is used for statistical evaluation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: One step production composite carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: N-doped CCQDs were prepared successfully and INS loading was done. The procedure was found to be simple and easily applicable. 0.4 g of citric acid monohydrate was used as a carbon source, and 0.005 g of l-cysteine was used for N-doping. The additional materials were not in high quantity therefore, the method was found to be cheap and quick. Blue fluorescent was observed under UV light (365 nm) at the end of the reaction (Fig. 1 ). This was accepted to be a quick initial confirmation because if something goes wrong the blue emission cannot be seen.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical appearances, particle size, and zeta potential
ce:section.ce:para: Prepared CCQDs were framed under HR-TEM after subsequent dilutions with water (Fig. 2 ). The bare CCQDs were seen individually among the other precipitated CCQDs. CCQDs were observed to be very small (less than 2 nm).
The zeta potential (ZP) and their particle sizes (PS) were measured. The PS was found to be increased after INS loading but it was not too effective and fluorescence was the same (Table 1 ).

The fluorescence was observed with prepared CCQDs. The bright blue emissions were a kind of initial proof that successful preparations can be made. Desired emissions can be obtained by exciting them with a correct light beam. 365 nm was found to be suitable and it was confirmed by a spectrofluorometer. The maximum emission was obtained with the excitation at around 365 nm. We also observed that using UV light with a higher wavelength (420 nm) causes emission light to shift to red.

The carbon source and the pH are found to be important and these parameters can change the doped atom and their surface characteristics. It was observed that the doped atom can be N if the medium is acidic. In the case of using a neutral or basic medium, the doped atom can be changed. The final composition and consequent fluorescence can be altered. The proposed mechanism for emitting blue or green light under UV is depicted in Fig. 3 .

The quinine sulfate solution was used as a standard. The intensity of obtained emission using CCQDs was found to be good and the calculated quantum yield was found to be good enough. The intensity-wavelength graphics of quinine sulfate and CCQDs were obtained (Fig. 4 A, B).

In our experiment the slope of the AUC intensity relation line of quinine sulfate was 880.84 (Fig. 5 A).; the slope of the AUC intensity relation line of CCQDs was 804.09; A = 0.999 (Fig. 5 B). The quantum yield was calculated as 91.2%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of insulin loading capacity of CCQD–INS–PEG formulations
ce:section.ce:para: The INS loading was calculated with the value of 94.634 ± 8.339% (SD, n = 14). This value shows that INS was successfully attached to the CCQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of drug release and release kinetics of CQD–INS–PEG formulations
ce:section.ce:para: The release profile is depicted in Fig. 6 . The release was found to be very fast at the beginning, after 30 min it was slow and reached the asymptotic value. INS was then started to degrade.

The kinetic of the release was found to be with the zero-order kinetics (r2 = 0.663, all points; r2 = 0.991 when only the linear part was considered first 60 min of the curve-). The r2 was 0.991 for first-order kinetic and 0.924 for Q√t kinetic. Other kinetics were also considered but none of them gave superior results.

The diabetic Wistar rats were used and the formulations were given. Streptozocin was used (40 mg/kg, IV) (Ilbasmis et al., 2012) and higher blood glucose levels were observed. Blood samples were taken from tail veins and analyzed by glucometer. The control group received nothing, the positive control group received INS solution subcutaneously, and the test group received INS-loaded CCQDs. Blood samples were then analyzed and compared. To understand the actual reason for the blood lowering effect, different INS doses (50 IU/kg and 100 IU/kg) were administered. Bare CCQDs were also given to the diabetic rats to understand any possible effects. Surprisingly blood glucose level was found to be lowered with the oral administration of bare CCQDs (without having INS). The isotonic saline solution was also tested to understand any negative effects (Fig. 7 ).
Blood INS levels of rats were also measured and very low INS levels were observed in diabetic animals but higher INS levels were observed in the INS-loaded CCQDs received group (Fig. 8 ).

A time-dependent degradation was observed for INS in the formulations when stored at high temperatures. The characterization parameters were tested during the stability tests. CCQDs formulations were kept at −20 °C, 5 o C, 25 o C, and 40 o C, and the shelf life was calculated. The relative humidity values were 60% and 75% for 25 o C, and 40 o C respectively. The observed parameters were given in Tables 2–5
and Figs. 9–11
. The shelf life of the formulation was found to be higher than 24 months when stored in deep freezer or in the refrigerator.

The prepared CCQDs were initially examined under UV light to ensure the existence of quantum dots. If CCQDs cannot be prepared correctly, the brightness of fluorescence and the color are changed. Any artifacts and damage on the surface of CCQDs and the particle size alter the color and brightness [43,44]. In classic quantum dots, the emission color is directly related to the size but in these CCQDs, the emission color is also affected by the roughness of the surface. When the emission color is related to the size in classic CCQDs the surface, artifacts on the surface and surface functionalization control the emission color. If the color is red the particle size becomes larger. In other words, bigger particles emit red light [9], but in CCQDs, bigger particles do not necessarily reflect red light. The emission is also related to the functional groups and the dopped atom present in the CCQDs structure. When l-cysteine was used to dopped nitrogen atoms to the CQDs structure, the orientation of atoms takes place in a specific way, and prepared CCQDs emits bright blue lights under UV (365 nm). Surprisingly when the same chemicals were used in the same amount in a neutral slightly basic medium, prepared CCQDs emitted bright green light under UV (365 nm).

The amount of the citric acid and l-cysteine were found to be important but not super critical. Higher amount of l-cysteine cause blurred solution because of the solubility of the l-cysteine. Undissolved and undoped molecules causes blurry solution. It is possible to remove undissolved big particles by filtration but it not recommended. Low amount of citric acid resulted in lower quantum yields when high amount of citric acid causes viscous and yellowish colored solution. The proposed and declared amounts of citric acid and l-cysteine are optimum values. The other ratios and amounts were tested but non gave superior result.

Previously, it was thought that the fluorescence was caused by surface passivation and the experiments were repeated by adding different substances. Ammonium fluoride, to phenylethylamine hydrochloride, was tried but none of them gave superior results [44]. These prepared CCQDs were found to be more suitable for INS administration and PEGylation. Because INS attachment and PEGylation were found to be easier because of the adsorptive nature of CCQDs. Methyl cellulose was used to stabilize CCQDs formulations by making the microenvironment of CCQDs more viscous and the aggregations or Oswalt ripening effect -surface interactions-can be reduced. This formulation was chosen as the best formulation. As a result, although a stable green fluorescence was obtained at neutral pH, it was understood that the CCQDs obtained in an acid medium emitting blue light were more stable and even smaller than green ones. The penetration and the targeting can be achieved by controlling the size of the penetrant. If the penetrant is in nano size, it is possible to get higher penetration and organelle. It is mentioned in the literature that QDs can be targeted to the mitochondria [9].

The pH of the solution should be neutral for INS. Therefore, the pH of the solution was adjusted to the neutral value. INS is a positively charged peptide. It has been observed that the prepared CCQDs are more positively charged in an acidic medium but they are negatively charged under neutral conditions. Negatively charged nanoparticles are known to be penetrating faster through the biological membranes [45,46]. Therefore, it was expected that CCQDs to penetrate faster and more if they are negatively charged (Table 1). It was indeed true and observed in our experiment that our prepared CCQDs are more penetrable through biological membranes under neutral conditions. As was mentioned, prepared CCQDs are quite small in the size and their particle size is good enough for faster penetration. Therefore, bare CCQDs penetration abilities were investigated. The dialysis membrane was used (12.000 Da), and very fast penetration was observed. The penetration of CCQDs immediately starts and equilibrates within four to 6 h when observed under UV. However, the pore size of the membrane plays an important role in the penetration. Smaller pore sizes limit the penetrations more. It is known that a dialysis membrane with a pore size of 3000 Da which has the smallest pore size having membrane (pore size is approximately 2.9 nm as indicated on the [47] may not be suitable for the penetration experiments. Because the size of prepared CCQDs is measured to be smaller than 2 nm and in this case, penetrations can be slowed down more by the membrane. If the pore size is calculated according to the literature, it can be found as 4.55 nm for 3000 Da [19,48]. Even if CCQDs can pass through the membrane it may be good to use a membrane with higher pore size. Therefore, using a dialysis membrane (12.000 Da cut-off) was found to be suitable. The final formulation with all excipients and INS was also tested and similar characteristics and tendencies were observed.

Quantum yield is an important parameter for characterizing the CCQDs. In the literature, quantum yield can be calculated from the standard fluorophore quinine-sulfate. Prepared CCQDs were found to emit quite bright blue light which is an indicator of high quantum yield. The quantum yield of prepared CCQDs was calculated as 91.2% and this result and the preparation technique were found to be successful.

INS loading to the CCQDs was achieved by nonionic adsorption. Carbon atoms in the structure of CCQDs are ready to adsorb material by van der Waals or π- π interactions [49]. These adsorption and electronic attractions are quite strong considering the very high surface area and being on the nanoscale. These forces are reported to be quite strong and capable enough in the literature and it has been used to formulate active chemical complexes. This is also used for QDs drug composites.
The best formula was the CCQDs (2.86 ± 1.048 nm) prepared from citric acid after the pH was 7, and the formula was finalized by adding INS, PEG, and lastly methylcellulose [49].

Drug dissolution from a dosage form or delivery system has been described by some kinetic models which include zero-order kinetics, first-order kinetics, Q√t, Higuchi model, and Hixson-Crowell. These mathematical models try to find a relationship between drug release and some parameters like polymer content, tortuosity, porosity, etc. Zero-order release kinetics refers to the process of constant drug release from a drug delivery system with mainly low soluble or controlled release drugs. The constant release is defined in this context as the same amount of drug release per unit of time. First-order release kinetic tries to find a relation between drug release and time with first order. Q√t kinetic indicates a diffusion-controlled release where the release is related to the squared of time. If it is not the case, drug release from matrices may involve processes of diffusion, erosion, and leaching or dissolution where the others represent a quite complex relationship between release and structure-based parameters. When the drug molecules were attached to the CCQDs, the release rate and kinetics can be altered. Therefore, the release kinetics of INS from CCQDs was investigated, and first order and Q√t kinetics were found to be dominant. In fact, our results indicate a biphasic release. The initial part shows an immediate release then a rather slow release takes place. It can be said that a first-order release dominates but a rapid release with a first-order can be the result of being a very small nanoparticle. INS molecules can be also reorganized when attached to the CCQDs surface. Because the electronic charge and 3D structure of INS peptides change these forces INS to be reorganized to reach the minimum energy state. In this reorganization, the volume and the surface area of the INS molecule can change. The release may be changing because of CCQDs. First-order and Q√t kinetics indicate that INS molecules can be released from the composite structure rather lately. This can also be accepted as another indication of INS CCQDs interaction/adsorption.

CCQDs-INS complex was tested on animals and decreased blood glucose levels were observed after oral administration. This was related to the proportion of INS in the formulation. In other words, it was dose-dependent. Lower glucose levels were observed with 100 IU/kg CCQDs-INS administration. Surprisingly lower blood glucose levels were observed when INS-free CCQDs were administered orally. Initially, it was thought that animals can get sodium chloride from the isotonic saline solution provided to give CCQDs and this can make them drink more water. This high-water intake can dilute the content of the glucose in the blood as a result blood glucose levels can be lowered. Animals were monitored carefully and their water intake was noted, there was no increased drinking or changed habits observed. The formulation component was also given to the animals without having INS and any reaction (no CCQDs). There were no changes in blood glucose levels. In all combinations tested, decreased blood glucose levels were observed when CCQDs were administered with or without INS. The blood-glucose-lowering effect was higher with INS presence (Fig. 6). It might be also possible that the process when giving the formulation by a gavage through the mouths of the animals may cause a kind of stress and this may affect the blood glucose level. Indeed, a slight fluctuation in blood glucose levels was noted. A solution containing only formulation components in the same ratio without INS was prepared and no quantum dot preparation process was applied (no quantum dots). This was applied orally to the animals and it was observed that the components of the formulation had no effect on the blood glucose level (Fig. 6). However, when the bare CCQDs without INS were given orally to animals using the same protocol, quite surprisingly, lowered blood glucose levels were observed. A lot of work has been done on this and no data has been discarded. The response of the animals to the INS was even tested by subdermal INS injections and animals were found to respond to INS. The bare CCQDs were subcutaneously injected into the animals and no changes were noted in blood glucose levels. It was confirmed that the INS is effective and CCQDs were not effective by subdermal injections. It was understood that our animals were just behaving normally but they were diabetic. In addition, bare CCQDs formulation was found to be similar or comparable with the CCQDs-INS formulation and INS injection. This shows that the prepared CCQDs-INS formulation can be even an alternative to INS injection. In a study performed on water-soluble ZnO quantum dots in the literature, it was reported that there was a structural defect in the cell when ZnO quantum dots interact with cells. It was compared with the standard samples and the quantum dots were found to be entered the cell by endocytosis and it was determined that the quantum dots preserved their structures and absorption spectra even in the cell [31]. In this study, they reported that quantum dots can actively enter living cells. In our study, it was observed that the thermodynamic activities of quantum dots are very high and they penetrated the tissues rapidly, even if they were administered orally. Statistical analysis was done and when only formulation components were administered orally to the animals the difference was significant (p < 0.05) at each time point comparing the oral CCQDs-INS administered group (50 or 100 IU/kg) was compared with the control group.

It was observed that the blood glucose level decreased more with the increase of the INS dose given to the animals (Fig. 6). The effect of formulation components on blood glucose levels was also tested by giving a solution containing the same level of formulation components to the animals but none gave any effect. It was reported in the literature that CCQDs can penetrate through multiple membrane barriers ranging from the cell/organelle to the tissue/organ level [50]. This report also indicates that positively charged quantum dots can penetrate much faster through cell membranes. Another study in the literature published in 2020 found that carbon quantum dots can be produced unintentionally during salmon cooking in an oven heated up to 200° [51]. It was also reported in the same literature that CQDs did not represent any histopathologic effect in rats with a dose of 2 g/kg. In 2015, there is another report was published related to pancreatic targeting of QDs. CdSe/ZnS QDs were reported to be targeted to the pancreas and lower blood glucose levels were found especially 3–4 weeks after the administration [27]. Glycolysis, targeting the pancreas and enhancing the metabolic activity in mitochondria was reported. If it is the case our results comply with that and lowered glucose levels are explainable.

Diabetes and related diseases arise from high blood glucose levels and the number of suffered patients has grown exponentially around the world. It should be also kept in mind that impaired glucose tolerance can be the result of a high blood glucose level which severely endangers people. Therefore, controlling blood glucose levels is of crucial importance in therapy. There are several drugs are available for reducing the blood glucose level. Among them, biguanides and sulfonylureas can decrease high blood glucose levels successfully but usually, some side effects can be seen [52,53]. Therefore, it seems that there is still a general need to develop an antihyperglycemic drug. CCQDs represented here appear to be a good alternative. CCQDs are a subclass of carbon materials, having been applied in various biological applications, including bioimaging, and drug delivery because of their low toxicity and well biocompatibility [54–56]. A carbon atom can have four bonds with other atoms therefore, it can be a chiral atom. Chirality is a common phenomenon and takes a critical role in physiological activities [57,58]. CCQDs are made of carbon therefore, some carbon atoms can be a chiral center in CCQDs. If it is the case this chirality can strongly affect their biological properties [59–61]. For instance, it was reported that l-CQDs prepared from l-cysteine showed upregulated glycolysis in the cellular energy metabolism of human bladder cancer T24 cells in the literature where d-CCQDs had no effect [40]. In our experiments, CCQDs were synthesized using l-cysteine and they showed quite a strong glycolytic effect. This is quite parallel to the literature findings and reports. Although it was not generally known and there are limited sources available, l-CCQDs are more effective in reducing blood glucose levels as mentioned here. We produced l-CCQDs by using l-cysteine as it was reported in the literature unintentionally at the beginning of the study and we observed quite a strong glycolytic effect. We first reported here that prepared CCQDs with or without INS effectively reduce blood glucose levels after oral administration.

CCQDs were made from organic compounds and they are actually graphene dots. In fact, traditionally organic materials are defined as living organisms, mostly containing C–H bonds, but later on, many non-living materials are included in the organic compounds containing C–H bonds. Most are combinations of a few of the lightest elements, particularly hydrogen, carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen. On the other hand, an inorganic compound is typically lacking C–H bonds. Mostly consist of heavy elements in the periodic table. In that sense, Graphene and resulting CCQDs may be categorized as organic materials. Inorganic nanoparticles are known to be more toxic. Although they may be an inorganic or organic material, they may have some higher toxicity on other cells and this should be studied.

CCQDS were prepared successfully. Various formulations can be prepared with CCQDs, their surface charge can be changed and they can be modified with PEG. CCQDs are shown to be a good carrier system for peptides. The fluorescence of CCQDs was shown and the calculation of quantum efficiency has been demonstrated and very high quantum efficiency has been achieved. Prepared CCQDs represent high thermodynamic activities and they were found to penetrate through membranes very quickly in vitro. For the first time in the literature, INS formulation was prepared with CCQDs, stability tests, and in vitro and in vivo experiments were performed, and lower blood glucose levels were obtained. It has been understood that CCQDs-INS are targeting pancreatic cells. It has been determined that CCQDs cause glycolysis very quickly and effectively by increasing metabolic activity and acting in the pancreas, thereby lowering blood glucose levels, but not showing high toxic effects in animals after oral administration.
